description: To perceive flavor through the tongue, or the sense by which flavors are known. 'Thorima' encompasses the five tastes—sweet, sour, salty, bitter, umami—and their infinite combinations. Tasting is intimate consumption, taking the world into the body to know it, the sense that guides nourishment.
sound_symbolism: The word begins with 'tho' /θo/, the dental fricative made with tongue near teeth—the very organ of taste positioned for its work. The 'o' rounds as the mouth receives. Then 'ri' rolls with the richness of flavor unfolding. Finally 'ma' closes with nasal satisfaction, the taste fully experienced.
grammatical_notes: As verb: 'mi themo thorima' (I taste something). As noun: 'wela thorima' (good taste). Can be used metaphorically for aesthetic judgment: 'ru wela thorima hie' (you have good taste).

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Mindful eating begins with tasting fully—present to each flavor, grateful for nourishment, aware of the chain from soil to tongue.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Taste guides survival—bitter warns of poison, sweet promises energy. Traditional food wisdom trusts the tongue's ancient knowledge.
